request.question: What year did Maurice win the award given to the  "player judged most valuable to his team" in the NHL?

title: Maurice Richard
The Sporting News Most Valuable Player Award
List of Brownlow Medal winners
Clive Churchill Medal
Tim Taylor Award (NCAA)
KBO League Most Valuable Player Award
Pavel Bure Most Exciting Player Award
Conn Smythe Trophy
ABA Playoffs Most Valuable Player Award
Hart Memorial Trophy
sentences: Joseph Henri Maurice "Rocket" Richard {'1': ", '2': ", '3': ", '4': "} ( ; ] ; August 4, 1921 – May 27, 2000) was a Canadian professional ice hockey player who played 18 seasons in the National Hockey League (NHL) for the Montreal Canadiens.
He was the first player in NHL history to score 50 goals in one season, accomplishing the feat in 50 games in 1944–45, and the first to reach 500 career goals.
Richard retired in 1960 as the league's all-time leader in goals with 544.
He won the Hart Trophy as the NHL's most valuable player in 1947, played in 13 All-Star Games and was named to 14 post-season NHL All-Star Teams, eight on the First-Team.
On January 1, 2017, in a ceremony prior to the Centennial Classic, Richard was part of the first group of players to be named one of the '100 Greatest NHL Players' in history.
"The Sporting News" established "The Sporting News" Most Valuable Player Award in 1929.
The award was given annually to the player judged by "TSN" baseball experts as being the most valuable in each league.
The awards were discontinued in 1946.
The Brownlow Medal (formally the Charles Brownlow Trophy) is an individual award given to the player judged fairest and best in the Australian Football League (AFL) during the regular season.
Determined by votes cast by the officiating umpires after each game, it is considered the highest honour for individual players in the AFL.
The Clive Churchill Medal is the award given to the player judged to be man-of-the-match in the National Rugby League's annual Grand Final.
The award was created to honour Clive Churchill, one of the greatest rugby league players in Australian history, following his death in 1985.

A prestigious honour in the NRL, the medal's recipient is chosen by the selectors of the Australian national team and announced and awarded to the player judged best and fairest on the ground at every post-grand final ceremony.
The Tim Taylor Award is an award given out to the NCAA Division I player judged to be the most outstanding freshman.
The annual award was first received by Andreas Nödl after the conclusion of the 2006–07 season.
The KBO League Most Valuable Player Award is given to the player judged the most valuable player in the Korea Baseball Organization (KBO) League.
The most recent winner is Dustin Nippert of the Doosan Bears.
The Pavel Bure Most Exciting Player Award is an annual award presented by the Vancouver Canucks to the player judged to be team's most exciting as voted by the fans.
It is one of six annual team awards presented to Canucks players, awarded on the last home game of the regular season.
Although the Canucks Media Guide does not recognize any recipients prior to the 1992–93 season, there is record of an annual winner every year since the Canucks' inaugural season in 1970.
Prior to the 2013-14 NHL season, the award was simply known as the Most Exciting Player Award.

On November 1, 2013, Canucks Sports & Entertainment announced that it would be renamed the Pavel Bure Most Exciting Player Award in honor of the team's first Hockey Hall of Fame inductee and five-time winner of the award, Pavel Bure.
The most recent recipient is Bo Horvat, who received the award for the second consecutive year in the 2016–17 NHL season.
The Conn Smythe Trophy (French: "Trophée Conn Smythe" ) is awarded annually to the player judged most valuable to his team (MVP) during the National Hockey League's (NHL) Stanley Cup playoffs.
It is named after Conn Smythe, the longtime owner, general manager, and head coach of the Toronto Maple Leafs.
The Conn Smythe Trophy has been awarded 48 times to 42 players since the 1964–65 NHL season.
Each year, at the conclusion of the final game of the Stanley Cup Final, members of the Professional Hockey Writers' Association vote to elect the player deserving of the trophy.

The trophy is handed out prior to the presentation of the Stanley Cup by the NHL Commissioner and only the winner is announced, in contrast to most of the other NHL awards which name three finalists and are presented at a ceremony.
The ABA Playoffs Most Valuable Player Award was an annual American Basketball Association (ABA) given in the ABA Playoffs.
The award was first awarded in the 1968 ABA Playoffs, and was retired as part of the ABA–NBA merger.
In sports, the player judged to be the most important to the team is the most valuable player (MVP).
The Hart Memorial Trophy, originally known as the Hart Trophy, is awarded annually to the "player judged most valuable to his team" in the National Hockey League (NHL).
The original trophy was donated to the league in 1923 by David Hart, the father of Cecil Hart, the longtime head coach of the Montreal Canadiens.
The Hart Trophy has been awarded 92 times to 56 different players since its beginnings in 1924.
Each year, members of the Professional Hockey Writers' Association vote to determine the player who was the most valuable to his team during the regular season.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question` and `passages`, produce the single field `summary`.

Goal: compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a short intermediate state that makes it easy for the next-hop agent to (a) see what facts are present per candidate/entity, (b) see what is ambiguous or missing, and (c) know 1–4 prioritized next-hop retrieval targets or concise queries. This module does not produce the final answer unless the provided passages already unambiguously contain every fact required to resolve the question's relation for every argument.

Procedure:
1) Decide whether the question's relation is semantically complete given the supplied passages. Treat the hop as INCOMPLETE if any of the following hold: a) the relation requires combining or comparing facts across pages (e.g., comparing birth dates, publication/founding years, ordinal ranks, confirming a named director among candidates) and the passages do not supply each required fact unambiguously for all relevant arguments; b) any relation argument is ambiguous or has multiple candidate pages or mentions in the passages; c) an expected candidate page or explicit fact for an argument is missing (e.g., only one side of a comparison is present). Do not trigger INCOMPLETE solely from surface tokens (years, digits) — base the decision on whether the relation's arguments are unambiguously resolved.

2) If the hop is COMPLETE (all required facts to resolve the relation for every argument are present unambiguously in the passages): produce a concise derived result in `summary`. This may be a short phrase or sentence that states the necessary fact(s) needed by the pipeline. Keep it minimal and grounded in the passages; you may include a 1–2 token citation (passage title) if helpful.

3) If the hop is INCOMPLETE or AMBIGUOUS: do NOT assert a final answer or perform the final comparison/selection. Instead emit a short structured report in `summary` containing these labeled elements (prefer bullets or a compact paragraph with explicit labels). Keep all text neutral and non-assertive.

  - evidence: For each named candidate/argument mentioned in the question or passages, list the candidate label and the exact passage title(s) that mention it plus a very short quoted snippet or explicit fact token found there (1–12 words). If a candidate named in the question has no grounded mention in any provided passage, explicitly mark it as NOT present under evidence.

  - ambiguities_or_missing: Explicitly name any ambiguities (e.g., multiple candidates for the same name, multiple pages that could be the intended entity) and any missing expected pages or facts.

  - missing_facts_or_bridge / bridge_entity_or_missing_fact: State exactly which fact(s) or disambiguation is needed to complete the relation. Use a slight conditional labeling here:
      * If the question names an entity that has no grounded mention in the provided passages and the unresolved gap is identity/disambiguation (you cannot map the question token to a single canonical entity needed to ground later facts), label the gap as `bridge_entity_or_missing_fact` and specify that the canonical/biography page for that named entity is required to resolve identity.
      * Otherwise (the missing premise is a concrete attribute/property such as a date, developer, layout engine, filmography item, opening-order token, etc.), use `missing_facts_or_bridge` and describe the exact attribute(s) required (e.g., "birth date for Person A", "developer of Product X"). Do not request the canonical biography page by default in this case; prefer an attribute-targeted retrieval first.

  - retrieval_direction: Provide 1–4 prioritized next-hop targets or concise queries to resolve the missing facts/ambiguities. Order targets by priority and keep them short. Apply this retrieval-priority conditional:
      * If the missing gap is identity/disambiguation (see bridge_entity_or_missing_fact above), put the candidate's canonical/biography page title as retrieval_direction #1 (exact title if known) and include 1–2 biography-style fallback queries (e.g., "<Name> biography", "<Name> official page") as subsequent targets.
      * If the missing gap is a specific attribute/property, put 1–2 concise attribute-focused queries first (e.g., "<Item> developer", "<Item> layout engine", "<Work B> publication year"). Include the canonical/biography page as a secondary target only if it might reasonably contain the attribute and identity resolution could still be needed.

Formatting and style constraints (apply to both COMPLETE and INCOMPLETE cases):
- The output must be a single `summary` field only. Do not emit other fields.
- In INCOMPLETE reports, use neutral, non-assertive phrasing. Do not produce final comparisons, numeric answers, or definitive selections (e.g., avoid "X is the oldest" or "Answer: 3").
- Keep the report compact and actionable (short labeled bullets or one concise paragraph). Avoid long prose.
- It is allowed and encouraged to quote short factual snippets from passages and cite passage titles to show grounding.
- Do not defer or mark INCOMPLETE when the passages already unambiguously provide all facts for the relation and its arguments.

Produce the `summary` content that follows these rules.

Semantic decision rule (when to emit a retrieval-intent query)

In all other cases (including birth-name mappings, page-title targets, non-temporal bridges, or when no usable disambiguator is available), do not append birth/date relation words or invent disambiguators; instead prefer the minimal visible-anchor form (page-title, name-pair, or person+institution) appropriate to the missing bridge.
- If summary_1 already names a confirmed bridge (a clearly identified, verified linking entity or unambiguous metadata that directly connects the question's pieces), you may produce a compact query to fetch supporting evidence, but do NOT produce an answer in the `query` field.
- If summary_1 explicitly states that the bridge cannot be recovered automatically (extreme ambiguity or user-local context requiring clarification), do NOT force a misleading retrieval query. In that case, set `query` to the single short token BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE (all caps, no punctuation) so downstream steps can prompt the user for clarification. Do not invent facts.

Query construction rules
- Output exactly one compact line of text as `query`. Keep it short and retrieval-focused (few keywords, no full sentences). Use visible anchors from `question` and `summary_1` and minimal retrieval keywords that express the missing relation (e.g., anchor1 anchor2 which artist recorded both; AlbumTitle album artist genre; 1991–92 NHL season regular season).

Use only the visible disambiguator wording; do not invent or normalize parentheticals.
- Relation-token selection rule: choose a single concise relation token that matches the question's relation (e.g., "born" or "birth year" only for temporal birth queries). Do not append relation tokens when the canonical retrieval strategy is to use a page-title, exact name-pair, or person+institution anchor.
- Do not include the final answer or explanatory text. If your natural impulse would be to write a declarative answer or a clarification sentence in `query`, instead convert that output into a compact retrieval-intent query that targets the missing bridge.
- Preserve semantic fidelity: base the query only on visible anchors and relations in `question` and `summary_1`. Do not invent hidden identifiers or assume unseen facts.

candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`. The `summary` must be a concise, post-retrieval integration of the evidence that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Follow these rules when composing `summary`:

Examples of acceptable explicit predicates include clear statements like 'X with Y', 'X is a member of Y', 'X was born DATE', 'X is a recipient of AWARD', 'X is also called Z', or 'X began tenure in YEAR'. The trigger requires that the passage (a) names the same entity token as in `context` or an alias/role/date that is corroborated by other attributes in `context`, and (b) explicitly asserts the relation or attribute needed by the question. Do not append when the passage only contains the attribute string without an explicit predicate, a nearby mention, an indirect implication, or when the link refers to a different named entity.

- Transitive alias/composition: Do not assert transitive equivalence unless every hop in the chain is explicitly stated as an equivalence in the supplied passages and each hop clearly refers to the same entity. If only a mediated or inferred chain exists (i.e., intermediate links are not explicit equivalences and identity-aligned), suppress the appended support and preserve uncertainty.

- When not to append: Do not append supporting sentences and instead return only the canonical label/token when any of the following apply: the question expects a normalized identifier or single token (examples: binary/multiple-choice label selection between named options, identification-only questions, or explicit token-extraction such as 'middle name'); or the `context` already contains a full, passage-consistent, multi-clause integrated answer. Permit an exception for identification/single-token/binary cases only when the question's semantics truly demand the relation/attribute (for example, determining an era boundary or tenure date) and a passage explicitly provides the necessary predicate linking the context entity to that temporal relation or attribute.

- Identity alignment and verification: Before appending, confirm that the fact in `passages` refers to the same entity referenced in `context` by matching names, corroborating aliases, roles, dates, or other attributes. If identity is ambiguous or unsupported, refrain from appending and preserve uncertainty instead. Avoid naive substring or proximity matches as sufficient evidence.

- Style and content constraints: Keep output concise. If appending, limit `summary` to 1–3 sentences total (the primary concise answer plus at most one short supporting sentence per relevant entity or one joint supporting sentence). Preserve the exact canonical label/token selected from `context`. Use a compact paraphrase grounded in passage language, avoid inventing facts or long verbatim quotations, and avoid misattributing attributes from different entities.

- If there is insufficient information in `context` and `passages` to resolve the needed relation/attribute, summarize the uncertainty succinctly rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Produce only the `summary` field as the integrated output string.
candidate.final_answer.predict:

Use only summary_1 and summary_2 as evidence. Treat summary_2 as the later, integrated evidence and prioritize it when reconciling information; use summary_1 only as the earlier, pre-second-hop context. Do not assume the final state is unresolved solely because summary_1 contains bridging or retrieval-oriented language.

Produce exactly one field, answer, whose value is the minimal final answer string that is fully supported by the visible summaries. The answer field must contain only that minimal plain-text string and nothing else (no explanation, no surrounding quotes, no JSON wrappers, no debugging or transport artifacts).

Decide whether the question's intent is binary (a truth-value / yes-no question) or non-binary (asking for a name, title, date, place, numeric count, selection, or other factual value). Make this decision semantically (based on the question's meaning), not by surface heuristics like token counts or punctuation.

Preserve multi-token formatted place names, parenthetical acronyms or alternates when they are present or when the question's intent relies on them.

- If the question's intent is binary AND both summary_1 and summary_2 unambiguously agree on the same truth-value, emit exactly one lowercase token: either yes or no (and nothing else) as the answer value.

- For all other question intents (non-binary), emit the minimal factual string supported by the summaries exactly as needed: preserve multi-token values, necessary punctuation, full date formats, and place formats (for example, full names, "City, State", or "Month Day, Year"), and do not truncate these to a single token.

If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not jointly support an unambiguous minimal answer, do not fabricate or over-assert; follow the system's established fallback behavior for ambiguous or unsupported questions.

Always forbid emitting serialized queries, JSON/dict-like structures, internal-debug wrappers, or other transport artifacts as the final answer. The answer must be plain text only.

Make all judgments about minimality and agreement based on the semantic content of summary_1 and summary_2, with priority to summary_2 when they differ.

Produce the answer value exactly as specified above in the answer field.

title: Maurice Richard

sentences: Joseph Henri Maurice "Rocket" Richard {'1': ", '2': ", '3': ", '4': "} ( ; ] ; August 4, 1921 – May 27, 2000) was a Canadian professional ice hockey player who played 18 seasons in the National Hockey League (NHL) for the Montreal Canadiens.
He was the first player in NHL history to score 50 goals in one season, accomplishing the feat in 50 games in 1944–45, and the first to reach 500 career goals.
Richard retired in 1960 as the league's all-time leader in goals with 544.
He won the Hart Trophy as the NHL's most valuable player in 1947, played in 13 All-Star Games and was named to 14 post-season NHL All-Star Teams, eight on the First-Team.
On January 1, 2017, in a ceremony prior to the Centennial Classic, Richard was part of the first group of players to be named one of the '100 Greatest NHL Players' in history.
"The Sporting News" established "The Sporting News" Most Valuable Player Award in 1929.
The award was given annually to the player judged by "TSN" baseball experts as being the most valuable in each league.
The awards were discontinued in 1946.
The Brownlow Medal (formally the Charles Brownlow Trophy) is an individual award given to the player judged fairest and best in the Australian Football League (AFL) during the regular season.
Determined by votes cast by the officiating umpires after each game, it is considered the highest honour for individual players in the AFL.
The Clive Churchill Medal is the award given to the player judged to be man-of-the-match in the National Rugby League's annual Grand Final.
The award was created to honour Clive Churchill, one of the greatest rugby league players in Australian history, following his death in 1985.

A prestigious honour in the NRL, the medal's recipient is chosen by the selectors of the Australian national team and announced and awarded to the player judged best and fairest on the ground at every post-grand final ceremony.
The Tim Taylor Award is an award given out to the NCAA Division I player judged to be the most outstanding freshman.
The annual award was first received by Andreas Nödl after the conclusion of the 2006–07 season.
The KBO League Most Valuable Player Award is given to the player judged the most valuable player in the Korea Baseball Organization (KBO) League.
The most recent winner is Dustin Nippert of the Doosan Bears.
The Pavel Bure Most Exciting Player Award is an annual award presented by the Vancouver Canucks to the player judged to be team's most exciting as voted by the fans.
It is one of six annual team awards presented to Canucks players, awarded on the last home game of the regular season.
Although the Canucks Media Guide does not recognize any recipients prior to the 1992–93 season, there is record of an annual winner every year since the Canucks' inaugural season in 1970.
Prior to the 2013-14 NHL season, the award was simply known as the Most Exciting Player Award.

On November 1, 2013, Canucks Sports & Entertainment announced that it would be renamed the Pavel Bure Most Exciting Player Award in honor of the team's first Hockey Hall of Fame inductee and five-time winner of the award, Pavel Bure.
The most recent recipient is Bo Horvat, who received the award for the second consecutive year in the 2016–17 NHL season.
The Conn Smythe Trophy (French: "Trophée Conn Smythe" ) is awarded annually to the player judged most valuable to his team (MVP) during the National Hockey League's (NHL) Stanley Cup playoffs.
It is named after Conn Smythe, the longtime owner, general manager, and head coach of the Toronto Maple Leafs.
The Conn Smythe Trophy has been awarded 48 times to 42 players since the 1964–65 NHL season.
Each year, at the conclusion of the final game of the Stanley Cup Final, members of the Professional Hockey Writers' Association vote to elect the player deserving of the trophy.

The trophy is handed out prior to the presentation of the Stanley Cup by the NHL Commissioner and only the winner is announced, in contrast to most of the other NHL awards which name three finalists and are presented at a ceremony.
The ABA Playoffs Most Valuable Player Award was an annual American Basketball Association (ABA) given in the ABA Playoffs.
The award was first awarded in the 1968 ABA Playoffs, and was retired as part of the ABA–NBA merger.
In sports, the player judged to be the most important to the team is the most valuable player (MVP).
The Hart Memorial Trophy, originally known as the Hart Trophy, is awarded annually to the "player judged most valuable to his team" in the National Hockey League (NHL).
The original trophy was donated to the league in 1923 by David Hart, the father of Cecil Hart, the longtime head coach of the Montreal Canadiens.
The Hart Trophy has been awarded 92 times to 56 different players since its beginnings in 1924.
Each year, members of the Professional Hockey Writers' Association vote to determine the player who was the most valuable to his team during the regular season.

response.hop1_query: What year did Maurice win the award given to the  "player judged most valuable to his team" in the NHL?
hop1_docs: Arena Football League Most Valuable Player Award | The Arena Football League Most Valuable Player Award (AFL MVP) is an award given by the Arena Football League (AFL) to the arena football player who is considered most valuable to his team in the AFL.  From 1996 to 2010, the League did not award an MVP.

Hart Memorial Trophy | The Hart Memorial Trophy, originally known as the Hart Trophy, is awarded annually to the "player judged most valuable to his team" in the National Hockey League (NHL).  The original trophy was donated to the league in 1923 by David Hart, the father of Cecil Hart, the longtime head coach of the Montreal Canadiens.  The Hart Trophy has been awarded 92 times to 56 different players since its beginnings in 1924.  Each year, members of the Professional Hockey Writers' Association vote to determine the player who was the most valuable to his team during the regular season.

ABA Playoffs Most Valuable Player Award | The ABA Playoffs Most Valuable Player Award was an annual American Basketball Association (ABA) given in the ABA Playoffs.  The award was first awarded in the 1968 ABA Playoffs, and was retired as part of the ABA–NBA merger.  In sports, the player judged to be the most important to the team is the most valuable player (MVP).

Conn Smythe Trophy | The Conn Smythe Trophy (French: "Trophée Conn Smythe" ) is awarded annually to the player judged most valuable to his team (MVP) during the National Hockey League's (NHL) Stanley Cup playoffs.  It is named after Conn Smythe, the longtime owner, general manager, and head coach of the Toronto Maple Leafs.  The Conn Smythe Trophy has been awarded 48 times to 42 players since the 1964–65 NHL season.  Each year, at the conclusion of the final game of the Stanley Cup Final, members of the Professional Hockey Writers' Association vote to elect the player deserving of the trophy.  The trophy is handed out prior to the presentation of the Stanley Cup by the NHL Commissioner and only the winner is announced, in contrast to most of the other NHL awards which name three finalists and are presented at a ceremony.

KBO League Most Valuable Player Award | The KBO League Most Valuable Player Award is given to the player judged the most valuable player in the Korea Baseball Organization (KBO) League.  The most recent winner is Dustin Nippert of the Doosan Bears.

The Sporting News Most Valuable Player Award | "The Sporting News" established "The Sporting News" Most Valuable Player Award in 1929.  The award was given annually to the player judged by "TSN" baseball experts as being the most valuable in each league.  The awards were discontinued in 1946.

ECHL awards | The ECHL presents numerous annual awards to recognize its teams, players, front office staff, and media contributors.  The first awards were given out in recognition of the achievements of the league's teams and players in the league's inaugural season of 1989 and included the Jack Riley Cup and Brabham Cup awarded to the league's teams and the Most Valuable Player, Playoffs Most Valuable Player, Rookie of the Year, Defenseman of the Year, Coach of the Year, and Leading Scorer awards to the league's players.  The league has added many other awards since the league's inception including, the Goaltender of the Year award in 1994, the Sportsmanship Award in 1997, separate titles for each of its conference playoff champions in 1998, and the Plus Performer Award in 2000.
hop1_titles: Arena Football League Most Valuable Player Award
ABA Playoffs Most Valuable Player Award
KBO League Most Valuable Player Award
The Sporting News Most Valuable Player Award

hop2_docs: Hart Memorial Trophy | The Hart Memorial Trophy, originally known as the Hart Trophy, is awarded annually to the "player judged most valuable to his team" in the National Hockey League (NHL).  The original trophy was donated to the league in 1923 by David Hart, the father of Cecil Hart, the longtime head coach of the Montreal Canadiens.  The Hart Trophy has been awarded 92 times to 56 different players since its beginnings in 1924.  Each year, members of the Professional Hockey Writers' Association vote to determine the player who was the most valuable to his team during the regular season.

Elmer Lach | Elmer James Lach ( , January 22, 1918 – April 4, 2015) was a Canadian professional ice hockey player who played 14 seasons for the Montreal Canadiens in the National Hockey League (NHL).  A centre, he was a member of the Punch line, along with Maurice Richard and Toe Blake.  Lach led the NHL in scoring twice, and was awarded the Hart Memorial Trophy in 1945 as the league's most valuable player.

Maurice "Rocket" Richard Trophy | The Maurice "Rocket" Richard Trophy, also known as the Maurice Richard Trophy, is awarded annually to the leading goal scorer in the National Hockey League (NHL).  It was donated to the NHL by the Montreal Canadiens in and is named in honour of legendary Montreal Canadiens right winger Maurice "Rocket" Richard.  It was first won by Teemu Selanne.  The current holder is Sidney Crosby of the Pittsburgh Penguins, who scored 44 goals during the season.

Maurice Richard | Joseph Henri Maurice "Rocket" Richard {'1': ", '2': ", '3': ", '4': "} ( ; ] ; August 4, 1921 – May 27, 2000) was a Canadian professional ice hockey player who played 18 seasons in the National Hockey League (NHL) for the Montreal Canadiens.  He was the first player in NHL history to score 50 goals in one season, accomplishing the feat in 50 games in 1944–45, and the first to reach 500 career goals.  Richard retired in 1960 as the league's all-time leader in goals with 544.  He won the Hart Trophy as the NHL's most valuable player in 1947, played in 13 All-Star Games and was named to 14 post-season NHL All-Star Teams, eight on the First-Team.  On January 1, 2017, in a ceremony prior to the Centennial Classic, Richard was part of the first group of players to be named one of the '100 Greatest NHL Players' in history.

Richard C. du Pont Memorial Trophy | The Richard C. du Pont Memorial Trophy is an American competitive award given annually by the Soaring Society of America to the U.S. National Open Class Soaring Champion, as determined at the annual U.S. National Open Class Soaring Championship.
Maurice Filion Trophy | The Maurice Filion Trophy is awarded annually to the general manager of the Year in the Quebec Major Junior Hockey League.  It was first awarded in 2005–06.

Maurice Prévost | Lucien Maurice Prévost, normally Maurice Prévost, (September 22, 1887 in Reims – November 27, 1952) in Neuilly-sur-Seine) was a French pioneer aviator, best remembered for winning the first Schneider Trophy race in 1913, and the Gordon Bennett Trophy the same year.
hop2_titles: Richard C. du Pont Memorial Trophy
response.summary_2: Maurice — Maurice "Rocket" Richard won the Hart Memorial Trophy (NHL MVP) in 1947.
